event: Born at 24 weeks + 3 days via emergent cesarean delivery
Management protocol for the extremely preterm infant was initiated
Developed late-onset sepsis secondary to Escherichia coli bacteremia
Required antimicrobial therapy with cefepime for 10 days
Removal of an adhesive patch located in the proximal third of the left arm
Intensive treatment by the wound care team with healings
Hydrating dermal wound dressings with sodium alginate and carboxymethylcellulose
Clinical deterioration consisting of thermic instability
Patient underwent skin biopsy for microbiological and pathological studies
Empiric antifungal treatment was initiated with liposomal amphotericin B
Fungal biomarkers such as serum galactomannan and 1,3 beta-D-glucan were not performed
General condition deteriorated progressively despite therapy with L-AmB and multisystemic support
The critical clinical condition did not allow extensive surgical debridement of the affected tissue
Fungal cultures obtained during skin biopsy revealed the growth of a mold that was classified by phenotypic characteristics as Rhizopus spp.
The histopathology report described the presence of broad aseptate hyphae with right angle branching, suggestive of Mucorales
Mass spectroscopy and PCR plus sequencing from culture identified Rhizopus arrhizus as the fungus responsible for the infection
Fungal blood cultures collected before starting L-AmB were negative
